name: John Levy
abstract: John Levy (April 11, 1912 – January 20, 2012) was an American jazz double-bassist and businessman.

interviews.title: John Levy interviewed by Monk Rowe, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995
John Levy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

concerts.label: Horizons in Jazz featuring Dizzy Gillespie, George Shearing, and Others

quotes.text: we got a job to go to 52nd Street and open in the Onyx Club. And in those days they gave you a long term deal, so the deal was for six months. And Jimmy Jones, I’m sort of ahead of myself, because actually Jimmy Jones joined the group — Raymond Walters was the first pianist with us, and he worked with us maybe in Chicago for maybe about two or three months. And then Jimmy Jones and I had been friends, I knew him as a player and I recommended that he come on with us. And that’s how the real Stuff Smith Trio got together. And then we came to New York and opened in the Onyx Club in August of 1944. And then I met Joe Glaser for the first time — this agent and everything. And as I said I developed an excellent relationship with him, and learned a lot about the inside of the business and how it all worked, which helped me later on when I finally went into that end of the business. We worked there at the Onyx Club — I guess we stayed there about a year. And I think I left, I was the first one to leave the trio. I went with Don Byas for a while, and then did a lot of recordings with Ink Spots and you name it, different jazz groups, and then Errol Garner. I made a series of records with Errol, just four sides actually, for Savoy Records with Errol Garner. And from that, one thing to the other until we get to 1948. In 1948 I got a call from Bobby Tucker to join him to work with Billie Holiday. And Billie Holiday had been in Lexington, Kentucky for a rehab for the dope. And she was coming out, and she was going to do a Carnegie Hall concert. And this was 1948. So we got together, and Denzel Best was the drummer. And we rehearsed out in New Jersey at Bobby Tucker’s house, where Billie Holiday was staying. And rehearsed for that. And I can’t remember the exact date in 1948 that we played Carnegie Hall, but we played it. It was my first time in Carnegie Hall. It was the first time that Carnegie Hall had ever had just a trio playing on stage, you know, so I mean the union made a ruling for the future that you’d have to have a minimum number of twelve musicians to play Carnegie Hall.
quotes.caption: John Levy recalls Billie Holiday's 1948 Carnegie Hall debut
quotes.doc_title: John Levy interviewed by Monk Rowe, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995

quotes.text: She didn’t get in touch with me at all. I knew Billie, because I’d seen her work before on 52nd Street. But it was through Bobby Tucker, who was her accompanist and who did the arrangements for her and worked for her. When she came out of Lexington, which was the rehab place while she was away for dope – when that was over, that period was over, and she was allowed to – out to work again, she came – stayed at Bobby’s home. She was very close. He was like family with her, Bobby Tucker. Bobby wanted to start rehearsing for a Carnegie Hall concert which was set for her by a promoter who was trying to get involved in managing her. She had been managed by Joe Glaser before she was incarcerated. When she came out, there was another person involved in the original Carnegie Hall concert, who was trying to take over Billie,
quotes.caption: Recalling Billie Holiday's post-rehab Carnegie Hall concert planning
quotes.doc_title: John Levy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Maria Schneider
about_quotes.evidence: You got John Levy to be your manager. ... I never in a million years thought he’d take me on. And I sat down with him ... I showed him my whole schedule for the year.
about_quotes.doc_title: Maria Schneider interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: John has Johnnie's been a mentor with me until I finally woke up and learned a few things. Because outside of his musicianship he had a great business -- his mind's eye saw business pretty clean, you know?

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: So he hired me... So he had a band. The band was what I just named: him and Nat, Sam Jones, Junior Mance, and me. We made an album called Sharpshooters.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Nancy Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: So the object of my strategy was, if I’m going to do this, the only person who I would trust to help and be there for me would be John Levy.
about_quotes.doc_title: Nancy Wilson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, November 16, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Norman Simmons
about_quotes.evidence: John Levy his manager called and we took the whole trio that I had... John Levy, his manager, is the one who literally hired me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Norman Simmons interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 27, 2007 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Orrin Keepnews
about_quotes.evidence: I remember this meeting involving Cannonball, Grauer, myself, and John Levy, who was Adderely’s manager. We signed Cannonball...
about_quotes.doc_title: Orrin Keepnews — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
